title: DecodeSession.push() and cancel() have no mutual-exclusion contract, enabling concurrent push+cancel without ordering guarantee
description: The DecodeSession interface exposes push(), close(), cancel(), and done() as independent Promise-returning methods. There is no documented or enforced exclusion between push() and cancel() — a caller can call push() and cancel() concurrently and the protocol contract does not define which wins or whether partial state is safe. Without a sequenced teardown guarantee in the interface contract, implementations may send decode_chunk after decode_cancel in-flight (the wire protocol has no idempotent cancel ack fence before chunks are re-used). This is a contract gap that concrete implementations must handle defensively but the interface gives them no hook to enforce it.

title: MsgDecodeChunk carries no sequence number, making reordering and lost-chunk detection impossible across worker post-message boundaries
description: MsgDecodeChunk has only sessionId and chunk — no sequence counter or monotonic offset. In Node.js worker_threads and browser Worker, postMessage calls are ordered per-port, so normal operation is safe. However, if a worker is recycled mid-session (crash/restart path) and chunks buffered in-flight are re-delivered via a new port, there is no way for the worker to detect that a chunk belongs to a dead session vs. the newly assigned one sharing the same sessionId string. A sequence number field would close this race without changing the happy path.

title: DecodeOptions.signal (AbortSignal) has no corresponding field in MsgDecodeStart, so the scheduler cannot forward abort to the worker
description: DecodeOptions carries an AbortSignal (line 81) for caller-driven cancellation. MsgDecodeStart (protocol.ts) has no signal-derived field — only a separate MsgDecodeCancel message. This means abort must be converted to cancel by a middleware layer; if that conversion is absent or racy (signal fires between push() calls), the worker never sees the cancellation until a separate cancel message arrives. The gap is in the interface contract: DecodeOptions does not document whether signal fires before push() returns are safe, or whether the abort arrives late. A `cancelReason` field on MsgDecodeStart (pre-populated from signal.reason at start time) and a documented signal→cancel forwarding requirement would close this.

title: No structured cancellation-token type: AbortSignal is used ad-hoc without a CancellationToken abstraction that can compose across session and scheduler layers
description: Both DecodeOptions and EncodeOptions accept a raw AbortSignal (lines 81, 216). The jxl-scheduler and jxl-session layers each need to check the signal independently. Without a shared CancellationToken interface (wrapping AbortSignal with a checked `isCancelled` property and composable child tokens), each layer reimplements signal-checking ad hoc, increasing the risk of a layer failing to propagate abort. Defining a CancellationToken in jxl-core would give all layers a uniform, testable abort contract.
code_excerpt:
signal?: AbortSignal;  // DecodeOptions line 81
  signal?: AbortSignal;  // EncodeOptions line 216

title: MsgDecodeCancel and MsgDecodePause share no epoch/generation field, so a stale pause ACK for a cancelled session is indistinguishable from a live one
description: When a session is cancelled while it is paused (decode_pause sent, decode_paused not yet received), the cancel message races the pause-ack. The protocol defines decode_cancelled and decode_paused as separate messages with only sessionId. If a decode_paused arrives after the session has been torn down and a new session reuses the same sessionId, the scheduler could incorrectly treat the stale ack as valid for the new session. Adding a generation counter or nonce to pause/resume/cancel messages would prevent this TOCTOU window.
